faqs.question: Do I need a permit to remodel my kitchen or bathroom?
faqs.answer: Yes, most kitchen and bathroom remodels require permits. If you're moving or adding plumbing fixtures, changing electrical wiring, or altering the structure (removing walls, adding windows), you'll need permits. A Combination Building Permit covers building, electrical, mechanical, and plumbing work in one application. Cosmetic changes like painting, replacing cabinets (same layout), or new flooring typically don't require permits.

faqs.question: Can I build an ADU (granny flat) on my property?
faqs.answer: Most likely yes. California state law (AB 2221, SB 897) has significantly streamlined ADU approvals. You can build one ADU and one Junior ADU (JADU) on most single-family lots. Key requirements: ADUs can be up to 1,200 sq ft for detached units, setbacks are reduced to 4 feet for detached ADUs, and the city must process your application within 60 days. Impact fees are waived for ADUs under 750 sq ft. No owner-occupancy requirement applies.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit to install solar panels?
faqs.answer: Yes, solar panel installations require a permit, but the process is streamlined. San Diego offers expedited review for residential solar installations. You'll need a Photovoltaic permit (or Electrical PV Combo permit that includes electrical work). Plans must show panel layout, electrical single-line diagram, and structural attachment details. Processing typically takes 1-3 weeks. AB 2188 requires cities to process residential solar applications within specified timeframes.

faqs.question: What work can I do WITHOUT a permit?
faqs.answer: Per San Diego Municipal Code §129.0203, the following work is generally exempt from permits: painting and wallpapering, carpeting and floor covering, replacing kitchen cabinets (same layout), replacing fixtures (same location), patios/decks under 30 inches above grade, fences/walls under 6 feet (3 feet in front yard), one-story detached accessory structures under 120 sq ft, and minor electrical work like replacing switches/outlets. Note: even exempt work must still comply with building codes.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get a building permit?
faqs.answer: Timelines vary by project complexity: No-plan permits (water heater, HVAC replacement): Same day to 1 week. Simple residential (solar, re-roof): 1-3 weeks. Residential additions/remodels: 4-8 weeks. New construction: 8-12+ weeks. ADUs: Maximum 60 days (state mandate). You can speed things up with Rapid Review (same day to 3 days, 50% surcharge) or Express Plan Check (5-10 days, 65% surcharge).

faqs.question: Do I need a permit to replace my water heater?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it's a simple No-Plan permit that can often be obtained the same day. If you're replacing with a like-for-like unit (same fuel type, similar capacity, same location), you just need a No-Plan Residential MEP permit. If you're changing fuel type (gas to electric), relocating the unit, or upgrading significantly, you may need a standard plumbing or mechanical permit with plans.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit to build a fence?
faqs.answer: It depends on the height and location. You do NOT need a permit for: fences/walls 6 feet or less in side/rear yards, or fences/walls 3 feet or less in front yards. You DO need a permit for: fences/walls over 6 feet, walls in the front yard over 3 feet, retaining walls over 3 feet, and any fence near a public right-of-way. Pool barriers have specific requirements regardless of height.

faqs.question: How do I check the status of my permit application?
faqs.answer: You can check permit status online through the City of San Diego's Development Services Department portal at https://www.sandiego.gov/development-services. You'll need your project ID or approval ID number. You can also call the Development Services Department at (619) 446-5000 or visit their office at 1222 First Avenue, San Diego, CA 92101.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit to convert my garage to a living space?
faqs.answer: Yes, converting a garage to a living space requires permits. This could qualify as a Junior ADU (JADU) if it's within the existing footprint of your home and under 500 sq ft. Otherwise, it's treated as a standard conversion requiring a building permit. Key requirements include: proper egress (windows/doors), insulation, electrical outlets, smoke detectors, and possibly parking replacement. If it qualifies as a JADU, the approval process is streamlined under state ADU law.

faqs.question: What is a Combination Building Permit and when do I need one?
faqs.answer: A Combination Building Permit bundles building, electrical, mechanical, and plumbing permits into a single application. It's ideal for projects that involve multiple trades, such as kitchen remodels, bathroom remodels, tenant improvements, and interior renovations. Benefits: single application, coordinated plan review, one set of fees, and simplified inspection scheduling. It's more efficient than applying for separate permits for each trade.

faqs.question: Can I do the work myself or do I need a licensed contractor?
faqs.answer: Property owners can do work on their own property (owner-builder) but must sign an Owner-Builder Declaration (form DS-16) acknowledging they understand building codes and will be responsible for the work. However, some work requires licensed professionals: electrical work over 50 volts requires a licensed electrician in many cases, plumbing work often requires a licensed plumber, and HVAC work typically requires a licensed contractor. For projects over $500, California law generally requires a licensed contractor unless you're an owner-builder.
